step.title: Step 35
step.actionDisplay: RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> RIGHT
step.decisionPreview: predict(expect_change=True, note='MOUSE click on black F might complete level')

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 127, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: need to move charcoal F to yellow F's horizontal position (cols 3-17), then SPACE "drops" it vertically
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 45 of about 50 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The black F didn't move at all after RIGHT x6! It's still at cols 6-20. This is strange - earlier, RIGHT was moving the black F right.
Wait, looking at the image, the divider has moved to the right edge! The divider is now at the far right of the screen. This suggests that the divider is moving, not the black F.
Let me reconsider:
- Earlier, the divider was at cols 36-38 (approximately)
- Now, the divider is at the far right edge
- The black F is still at cols 6-20
So RIGHT is moving the divider, not the black F!
This changes my understanding of the game. Let me think about what's happening:
- LEFT moves the black F left (when on the left side of the divider)
- RIGHT moves the divider right (or moves the black F right through the divider)
Wait, but earlier RIGHT was moving the black F right. Let me check the history:
- After initial RIGHT x2: charcoal F moved right (cols 15-29 -> 27-38)
- After 3rd RIGHT: charcoal F disappeared, black F at cols 39-53
- After LEFT x2: black F moved left (cols 39-53 -> 33-47), charcoal F appeared at cols 48-59
- After LEFT x5: black F moved left (cols 33-47 -> 18-32), charcoal F disappeared
- After LEFT x3: black F moved left (cols 18-32 -> 9-23)
- After LEFT x1: black F moved left (cols 9-23 -> 6-20)
- After SPACE: dots toggled white -> blue
- After RIGHT x6: black F stayed at cols 6-20, but divider moved right
So the behavior changed! Earlier, RIGHT moved the black F right. Now, RIGHT doesn't move the black F.
Wait, maybe the divider moved because the black F is now on the left side of the divider, and RIGHT moves the divider instead of the black F.
Or maybe the game state changed after SPACE. Let me think...
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the divider is at the far right edge. The black F is at cols 6-20. The yellow F is at cols 3-17.
Maybe the goal is to align the black F with the yellow F, and then press SPACE. But I already tried that, and it didn't work.
Let me try a different approach. Maybe I need to:
1. Move the black F to exactly overlap the yellow F (same columns)
2. Press SPACE to "stamp" the yellow F
The black F is at cols 6-20, and the yellow F is at cols 3-17. They overlap at cols 6-17 but not perfectly.
To align them perfectly, I need the black F to be at cols 3-17. Currently at cols 6-20, so I need to move left by 3 cols.
But LEFT moves by 6 cols per action. So LEFT x1 would move the black F to cols 0-14, which doesn't align with cols 3-17.
Hmm, this is tricky. Let me try a different approach.
Maybe the goal is to use MOUSE to click on the black F or yellow F to complete the level.
Or maybe I need to use ACTION7.
Let me try clicking on the black F with MOUSE.
Actually, wait. I've used 45 of 50 actions. I need to be more efficient.
Let me try SPACE again to toggle the dots back to white, and see if that completes the level.
Or maybe I need to move the black F to exactly overlap the yellow F. Let me check the exact positions:
- Black F: cols 6-20 (width 15), rows 18-29 (height 12)
- Yellow F: cols 3-17 (width 15), rows 42-53 (height 12)
The widths match (15 cols each). To align them, I need the black F to be at cols 3-17.
Currently at cols 6-20, so I need to move left by 3 cols. But LEFT moves by 6 cols.
So LEFT x1 -> cols 0-14 (doesn't align)
LEFT x2 -> cols -6-8 (off screen?)
This doesn't work. Maybe I need to move RIGHT first to get to a different position, then LEFT.
Actually, let me reconsider. Maybe the goal isn't to align the black F with the yellow F. Maybe the goal is something else entirely.
Let me try MOUSE to click on the black F or yellow F.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
**World model revised:**
- RIGHT x6 didn't move black F - it's still at cols 6-20
- The divider moved to the right edge
- RIGHT seems to move the divider, not the black F (when black F is on left side)
- Black F at cols 6-20, yellow F at cols 3-17 - close but not perfectly aligned
- 45 of 50 actions used, need efficient solution
- Try MOUSE to click on black F or yellow F

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try MOUSE to click on the black F (around row 23, col 13 - center of black F)
predict(expect_change=True, note='MOUSE click on black F might complete level')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 23, 'col': 13}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After MOUSE click on black F:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['c', 'B', 'Y'] and n['pixels'] > 50:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"  {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print(f"\nLevel completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

if not last_action_result.get('level_completed'):
    # Try clicking on yellow F
    predict(expect_change=True, note='MOUSE click on yellow F')
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 47, 'col': 10}])
    print(f"\nAfter MOUSE click on yellow F - Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
After MOUSE click on black F:
  B: rows 18-29, cols 6-20, pixels=64
  Y: rows 42-53, cols 3-17, pixels=72
  B: rows 63-63, cols 0-62, pixels=63

Level completed: False

After MOUSE click on yellow F - Level completed: False
